coredata.dc:title: PANI doped with carbon quantum dots for the asymmetric high-performance solid-state electrochromic supercapacitor
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Biao
Yang, Qin
Yang, Yuanhaobo
Chen, Jia
Yan, Bin
Gu, Yingchun
Fu, Runfang
Chen, Sheng
coredata.dc:description: The growing trend towards more intelligent technological products demands greater functional integration. The electrochromic supercapacitor (ECSC), which combines multicolor electrochromic behavior with energy storage, enhances user experience by visualizing the device's energy status. A key challenge for practical applications is to develop scalable, low-cost methods for producing high-performance electrochromic supercapacitors. In this work, we synthesize the polyaniline (PANI) doped with carbon quantum dots, which exhibits excellent capacitive and electrochromic performance, and employ a spray-coating technique to fabricate the working electrode. Coupled with a transparent V2O5@PEDOT layer as the ion-storage counter electrode, a novel asymmetric solid-state ECSC is assembled. The ECSC demonstrates high energy/power density (51.87 μWcm−2/4.02 μWhcm−2–980.41 μWcm−2/2.914 μWhcm−2), fast switching times (tc = 0.75 s, tb = 2.61 s), outstanding coloring efficiency (221.6 cm2 C−1) and cycling stability (over 5000 cycles). The distinct color transitions enable intuitive visual assessment of the device's energy storage level. Moreover, the spray-coating method facilitates large-area and patternable production, offering a promising route for developing next-generation smart and portable multifunctional electronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polyaniline
Asymmetric electrochromic supercapacitors
Spray-coating
Patterned fabrication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical structure of CQDs and CQD@PANI
Electrochemical and electrochromic properties of CQD@PANI
Electrochemical and electrochromic properties of ECSC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Sichuan Province Science and Technology Foundation (2020YFG0094). The authors would like to thank Hui Wang in the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for SEM tests, and Shiyanjia Lab (https://www.shiyanjia.com/) for the XRD analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process for the asymmetric ECSC.
SEM images of pure PANI (a), CQD@PANI-1 (b), CQD@PANI-2 (c), CQD@PANI-3 (d), and schematic of the polymerization process of CQD@PANI nanocomposites(e).

Electrochemical performance of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode: CV curves at various scan rates (a), capacitance contribution analysis at 10 mV/s (b), contribution ratios of the capacitance and diffusion-controlled capacity at different scan rates (c), GCD curves at various current densities (d), specific capacitances at various current densities (e), and cycling stability at 10 A/g (f).

Optical and electrochromic properties of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode: transmittance curves at bleached and colored states (a), photographs and L∗ a∗ b∗ values (b), CIE chrominance coordinates (c), chronoamperometry curves and in-situ optical transmittance curves (d), coloration efficiency (e), and GCD curves and the in-situ spectrum (f).

Electrochemical characterization of the ECSC device: CV curves of the CQD@PANI-2 and V2O5@PEDOT electrodes (a), CV curves in different voltage windows (b), CV curves from 0 V to 1.2 V at various scan rates (c), GCD curves at various current densities (d), corresponding area capacitance at various current densities (e), and cycling stability of area capacitance at 10 A/g (f).

Optical and performance analysis of the ECSC device: photographs and transmittance curves at various potential (a), chronoamperometry curve and in-situ optical transmittance (b), coloration efficiency (c), Ragone plots comparing areal power/energy densities with reported devices (d), CIE chrominance coordinates (e), and GCD curves and ΔE∗ at 700 nm (f).

Demonstration of patterned ECSC device: photographs of the patterned ECSC (a), red LEDs powered by the ECSC (b), and the patterned ECSC at various potentials (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: PANI doped with carbon quantum dots for the asymmetric high-performance solid-state electrochromic supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, National Engineering Laboratory for Clean Technology of Leather Manufacture, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Biomass Science and Engineering
National Engineering Laboratory for Clean Technology of Leather Manufacture

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Biomass Science and Engineering, National Engineering Laboratory for Clean Technology of Leather Manufacture, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The growing trend towards more intelligent technological products demands greater functional integration. The electrochromic supercapacitor (ECSC), which combines multicolor electrochromic behavior with energy storage, enhances user experience by visualizing the device's energy status. A key challenge for practical applications is to develop scalable, low-cost methods for producing high-performance electrochromic supercapacitors. In this work, we synthesize the polyaniline (PANI) doped with carbon quantum dots, which exhibits excellent capacitive and electrochromic performance, and employ a spray-coating technique to fabricate the working electrode. Coupled with a transparent V2O5@PEDOT layer as the ion-storage counter electrode, a novel asymmetric solid-state ECSC is assembled. The ECSC demonstrates high energy/power density (51.87 μWcm−2/4.02 μWhcm−2–980.41 μWcm−2/2.914 μWhcm−2), fast switching times (tc = 0.75 s, tb = 2.61 s), outstanding coloring efficiency (221.6 cm2 C−1) and cycling stability (over 5000 cycles). The distinct color transitions enable intuitive visual assessment of the device's energy storage level. Moreover, the spray-coating method facilitates large-area and patternable production, offering a promising route for developing next-generation smart and portable multifunctional electronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) get the upper hand over conventional batteries, possessing high-energy density, fast charging-discharging rates, superior cycling stability, and long-term durability, making them a promising next-generation clean energy solution [1–3]. Currently, SCs are widely used in hybrid electric vehicles, uninterruptible power supplies and as battery supplements in various public and industrial applications [4]. With the growing demand for intelligent and wearable electronic devices, integrating multifunctional capabilities into supercapacitors has emerged as a key research direction. One promising approach is the integration of electrochromism into supercapacitors. The electrochromic supercapacitor (ECSC) leverages the alignment of color changes with charge injection and extraction during the charging-discharging process, providing visual feedback on the energy status [5,6]. This unique feature not only enhances usability but also prevents overcharging, thereby improving the safety and practicality of the ECSC.

A critical factor in ECSC performance is the electrode material, which serves both as the electrochromic layer and the energy storage medium. Although significant progress has been made in developing multifunctional materials, many of the current ECSC designs still face challenges in terms of fabrication complexity, cost of production, and scalability owing to the use of expensive production equipment and raw materials [7]. Hence, it is crucial to design novel bifunctional materials with enhanced properties through simple and efficient preparation methods.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have obtained considerable attention as prospective candidates for supercapacitor materials due to their large surface area, outstanding conductivity, exceptional chemical stabilities, low-budget and simple preparation technology [8]. Particularly, doping CQDs with heteroatoms (such as oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, or sulfur) can significantly enhance their electrochemical performance. Nitrogen doping, in particular, has been shown to improve the conductivity, capacitive performance, rate capability and cycling life of supercapacitor devices [9]. For instance, Hsiao et al. synthesized N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) by means of one-step thermal treatment process, producing a carbon black/NCQD composite with outstanding conductivity and cycling stability [10]. Similarly, Ganganboina et al. anchored N-doped graphene quantum dots onto Fe3O4-halloysite nanotubes enhancing electron and electrolyte ion transport, and achieving high specific capacitance (418 F/g) and rate performance [11].

Polyaniline (PANI), a widely studied conductive polymer, is also recognized for its potential in electrochromic and capacitive application. However, traditional PANI materials face limitations in advanced ECSC due to their dense ion transport layer, which restricts practical capacitance [12]. To overcome these limitations, combining PANI with other capacitive materials like CQDs can create hierarchical structures or modify its conjugate structure, enhancing electrochemical performance [13].

Herein, a polyaniline doped with carbon quantum dots (CQD@PANI) nanocomposite electrode material was synthesized through in-situ oxidative polymerization. Compared with pure PANI, CQD@PANI nanocomposite exhibits a regular fibrous structure, facilitating film formation and the development of a three-dimensional porous conductive network. This nanocomposite not only improves electrochromic behavior but also enhances energy storage performance. Coupled with the transparent vanadium pentoxide@poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (V2O5@PEDOT) counter electrode, the asymmetric ECSC demonstrates superior energy storage properties, high energy/power density, fast electrochromic response time and excellent cycling stability. Notably, the spray-coating technique enables large-area, patterned fabrication of electrodes, offering a scalable and economical approach for the fabrication of upcoming portable and intelligent electronic devices.

Aniline (An), ammonium persulfate (APS), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), citric acid (CA), ethylenediamine (EDA), and propylene carbonate (PC) were bought from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd., and used without further purification. Lithium perchlorate (LiClO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) was obtained from Suzhou Yangfan New Material Co., Ltd. The ITO/glass used as substrate was obtained by Huanan Xiangcheng Technology Company Limited (sheet resistance 8.0 Ω/sq, 3 cm × 2 cm in size, and 1.1 mm in thickness). The ITO/glass was sequentially cleaned by ultrasonic processing with acetone, ethanol, and deionized water. All experiments were conducted using deionized water.

The synthesis of the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) referred to the reported method with moderate adjustment [14]. Citric acid (0.5 g) and ethylenediamine (150 μL) were added to deionized water (5 mL) and dissolved by ultrasonic processing for 10 min. The mixed solution was then added into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. The obtained solution cooled to 20 °C underwent centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min to remove agglomerate from it. The obtained solution utilized a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (Spectra/Por, MWCO = 100–500) for 24 h to remove low molecular weight compounds. The obtained CQDs solution was further concentrated to a solid content of 10 mg/mL for subsequent use.

To synthesize the CQD@PANI nanocomposite, CTAB (0.124 g) was dissolved in 1 M HCl solution (30 mL) by ultrasonic processing. Then, varying volumes of the above obtained CQDs solution (0.1 mL, 0.2 mL, 0.3 mL) were dispersed in the mixture. Aniline (0.08 g) was subsequently added dropwise, and the mixture was continuously stirred at 0–5 °C for 30 min. Finally, APS (0.171 g) in 1 M HCl solution (10 mL) was gradually dripped into the mixed solution, and the mixed solution constantly stirred for 24 h. The correspondingly obtained solution was centrifugated with deionized water and ethanol and the nanocomposite redissolved in deionized water for further usage. Depending on the amount of CQD (0.1 mL, 0.2 mL, 0.3 mL), the composites were labeled CQD@PANI-1, CQD@PANI-2, and CQD@PANI-3, respectively. CQD@PANI-2 exhibiting the best overall performance was selected for detailed characterization.

The cleaned ITO/glass, known for its high transmittance and electrical conductivity, was served as the substrate material. The obtained CQD@PANI aqueous dispersion (0.2 wt%) was sprayed onto the ITO/glass on a heating plate set at 100 °C and then dried at 60 °C for 2 h. The working area of the electrode was 2 cm × 2 cm, with a loading density of 0.12 mg/cm2.

The V2O5@PEDOT composite, serving as the transparent ion-storage layer, was synthesized following a previously reported procedure with some modifications [15]. V2O5 (1.5 g) powder was dispersed into 1 M NaCl aqueous solution (50 mL), and then EDOT monomer (0.75 mL) was slowly dripped into the mixed solution. The mixture was continually stirred at 500 rpm for 7 days, during which the color gradually changed from yellow to dark green. Subsequently, the obtained V2O5@PEDOT nanowires were centrifugated, washed with deionized water for 5 times and sonicated for 30 min. The resulting V2O5@PEDOT dispersion was sprayed onto the ITO/glass substrates on a heating plate set at 100 °C and dried at 60 °C for 2 h. The ECSC was assembled in a sandwich structure using the CQD@PANI electrode, a 1 M LiClO4/PC/PMMA gel electrolyte, and the V2O5@PEDOT electrode. The process diagram regarding of fabrication of the asymmetric ECSC was shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphology of the samples was examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FlexSEM 1000 II) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (Talos F200i). The chemical structure of the nanocomposites was investigated by Fourier transform spectra (FTIR, FT-IR6600). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were assessed by Bruker D8X-ray diffractometer (λ = 0.154 nm). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) transmittance curves were obtained via an Thermo Scientific Evolution UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer.

The electrochromic and electrochemical properties of the CQD@PANI and V2O5@PEDOT electrodes were measured using a Chi 660 electrochemical workstation with a custom-built colorimetric device (50 mm × 45 mm × 50 mm). A three-electrode system was employed, with the CQD@PANI or V2O5@PEDOT electrode (the working electrode), a platinum sheet (the counter electrode), and a silver electrode (the reference electrode). The electrolyte solution used was 1 M LiClO4 in PC. Detailed calculation methods for the electrochemical and electrochromic properties were listed in the Supporting Information.

The CQDs were prepared using a hydrothermal method, as reported previously. TEM image of the CQDs aqueous dispersion and the corresponding size distribution histogram are shown in Fig. S1. The basic morphology of CQDs is successfully confirmed and CQDs with the size range of 3∼7 nm (the average value approached 5.1 nm) are uniformly distributed without aggregates.

Fig. 2 a–d presents the SEM images of both pure PANI and the various CQD@PANI nanocomposites. The surface of pure PANI membrane appears relatively compact, with few micropores. This dense morphology likely hinders electrolyte ion transport and leads to volume expansion of the conductive film during redox reactions, causing delamination from the ITO substrate. These structural issues negatively impact the electrochromic and capacitive properties of pure PANI (Fig. 2a).

In contrast, the CQD@PANI nanocomposites exhibit an interesting fibrous structure. CQD@PANI-1 displays predominantly fibrous morphology, though some areas still show aggregations (Fig. 2b). The CQD@PANI-2 sample develops more ordered, interwoven fibrous structure, forming a three-dimensional conductive network (Fig. 2c). Although CQD@PANI-3 has a similar fibrous appearance, it exhibits more irregularities and significant aggregations (Fig. 2d). To further investigate the morphology of the nanocomposite, Fig. S2 exhibits the TEM image of CQD@PANI-2. The nanocomposite demonstrates an elongated fibrous structure with occasional branching, and the width of the fibers ranged from 50 to 100 nm.

The presence of CQDs in the reaction mixture served as nucleation sites for PANI chain growth, with the covalent anchoring of PANI chains on the CQDs surface, promotes the formation of specific shapes (Fig. 2e). The limited amount of CTAB presented in the reaction system facilitates the formation of fibrous structured micelles by cationic radicals of aniline dimers, guiding the growth of fibrous PANI nanostructures [16,17]. These factors contribute to the ordered fibrous structure observed in the CQD@PANI composites. In the case of CQD@PANI-1, when the concentration of CQDs is lower in the reaction system, most aniline molecules grow preferentially on the surface of CQDs. However, excess aniline molecules undergo disordered auto-polymerization, leading to some aggregations in the structure [18]. Additionally, higher concentration of CQDs in CQD@PANI-3 causes the CQDs to aggregate, resulting in irregular polymer growth. CQD@PANI-2, with an optimized proportion of CQDs to aniline, displays a highly uniform fibrous structure, which is favorable for forming a stable and conductive film on ITO substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of CQDs and CQD@PANI
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were obtained through the pyrolysis of ethylenediamine and citric acid, resulting in a highly stable aqueous dispersion with abundant −COOH and −NH3 reactive functional groups on their surfaces [19]. Fig. S3 presents the stable CQDs aqueous dispersion, which exhibits blue fluorescence under excitation at 345 nm, along with the corresponding characteristic absorption peaks at 234 nm and 345 nm, consistent with the previously reported result [20].

The FTIR spectrum of CQDs, shown in Fig. S4a, provides related information concerning its surface functional groups. The peaks at 3440 and 1600 cm−1 assign to the stretching and bending vibrations of N−H bond, respectively. The peak at 3440 cm−1 also includes contributions from the stretching vibration of O−H, indicating that the CQDs possessed both O−H and N−H bonds, which effectively improves their hydrophilicity and dispersion stability. In addition, the peaks at 1700 cm−1 and 1200 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C−N bonds, respectively. These results confirm that the CQDs were abundant in functional groups, ensuring their successful preparation.

In contrast, the FTIR spectrum of the CQD@PANI-2 nanocomposite closely resembles that of pure PANI, with the original signals of CQDs largely concealed after in-situ polymerization. This indicates that CQDs are relatively small and used in small quantities, and embedded within the nanocomposite matrix, making their characteristic signals difficult to detect. The broad peak at 3426 cm−1 in both CQD@PANI-2 and pure PANI is attributed to the stretching vibrations of N−H and O−H. The intensity peaks at 1585 cm−1 and 1496 cm−1 assign to the quinoid and benzenoid ring structures in PANI. Additionally, bands at 1296 cm−1 and 1138 cm−1 ascribe to the stretching vibrations of C−N−C, characteristic of PANI.

Raman spectroscopy was further used to explore the interaction between CQDs and PANI. Fig. S4b compares the Raman spectra of pure PANI and CQD@PANI-2. In pure PANI, characteristic peaks are observed (Fig. 4e) at 1170 cm−1 (bending vibration of the C−H), 1495 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C=N), and 1600 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C=C in the quinoid ring). The peaks at 1226 cm−1 and 1645 cm−1 ascribe to the bending vibration of C−H and stretching vibration of C−C in the benzenoid ring, respectively. In CQD@PANI-2 sample, these peaks exhibit slight shifting, indicating interactions between CQDs and PANI. The bending vibration of C−H at 1170 cm−1 shifts to 1182 cm−1, while the stretching vibration band of C−N•+ at 1338 cm−1 shifts to1343 cm−1, suggesting a transition from quinoid to benzenoid ring segments during the doping process. The peak at 1505 cm−1 shifts to 1517 cm−1, likely due to coupling with the C=N stretching vibration in imine groups. Furthermore, the characteristic peak at 1598 cm−1 shifts to 1609 cm−1, illustrating that CQD@PANI in a higher doping state is beneficial to enhance its electrochemical properties [21].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and electrochromic properties of CQD@PANI
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the fundamental electrochemical and electrochromic performance of pure PANI and the three CQD@PANI nanocomposites, galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) tests were conducted. Fig. S5a exhibits the GCD curves, showing that CQD@PANI-2 presents the longest discharge time with a specific capacitance of 398.2 F/g, indicating exceptional capacitive performance. CQD@PANI-3 follows with a specific capacitance of 338.6 F/g. As discussed previously, the agglomeration in CQD@PANI-1 hinder ion and electron diffusion, resulting in internal resistance and a noticeable voltage drop, as seen in its GCD curves. Consequently, CQD@PANI-1 shows lower capacitance (compared to CQD@PANI-2 and -3) and a reduced working voltage range. Pure PANI exhibits the shortest discharge time and the lowest specific capacitance, demonstrating that the CQDs doping significantly alters PANI's conjugated structure, effectively improves its electrochemical performance.

Fig. S5b presents the capacitance of the diverse samples under varying current densities. The capacitance retention rates of samples at 10 A/g are listed in Table S1. CQD@PANI-2 demonstrates the highest retention rate of 80.1 %, reflecting its excellent rate capability, while CQD@PANI-3 also performs well with 75.2 %. In contrast, CQD@PANI-1 and pure PANI exhibit lower retention rates of 65.8 % and 45.7 %, respectively, due to their higher internal resistances and voltage drops during rapid charging and discharging cycles [22].

In addition to their electrochemical properties, their electrochromic performance of the nanocomposites was evaluated as well. CQD@PANI-2 exhibits an optical contrast of 65 % (Fig. S5c), which is higher than CQD@PANI-1, CQD@PANI-3, and pure PANI. Since the redox behaviors of conductive polymers influences both their electrochromic and capacitive properties, a higher degree of redox results in better performance in both areas [23]. Therefore, CQD@PANI-2 with its superior specific capacitance, also demonstrates enhanced electrochromic properties.

Switching time is a critical factor for practical applications of electrochromic materials, as it reflects the structural characteristics and the efficiency of charge and ion transport [24]. Fig. S5d and Table S1 summarizes the coloring time (Tc) and bleaching time (Tb) of the samples. CQD@PANI-2 shows the fastest switching times (Tc = 1.67 s, Tb = 1.45 s) due to its well-organized fibrous structure, which forms a porous, three-dimensional conductive network. This structure efficiently reduces the diffusion distances for electrons and ions, alleviates mechanical stress during cycling, and provides additional storage sites [25]. These factors collectively enable faster and more efficient redox processes. In addition, the diffusion coefficient (D) of CQD@PANI-2 and pure PANI was calculated using the Randles-Sevcik equation to accurately estimate the mobility of ion in the electrochemical reaction [26]. According to the CV curves at various scan rates (Fig. 3 a, S6), the peak current (ip) and the sqrt of scan rates exhibit a linear relationship as shown in Fig. S7. The diffusion coefficient is further obtained via the Randles-Sevcik equation [27]. The diffusion coefficient of the CQD@PANI-2 materials at diverse redox peaks is one order of magnitude higher than that of pure PANI (Fig. S8), evidently proving a rapid diffusion behavior of the CQD@PANI-2 with fibrous morphology during the electrochemical reaction. Overall, the introduction of CQDs significantly enhances both the electrochemical and electrochromic performance of PANI, with CQD@PANI-2 exhibiting the best properties across all tests, making it the primary material for subsequent investigations.

The electrochemical and electrochromic properties of CQD@PANI-2 were further explored. Fig. 3a showcases the cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at various scan rates, where two distinct pairs redox peaks are observed at approximately 0.31 V/0.75 V and −0.41 V/−0.10 V at 100 mV/s. Two distinct pairs of redox peaks are associated with the transformation between leucoemeraldine base (LB)/emeraldine salt (ES) and emeraldine base (EB)/pernigraniline salt (PS), respectively [28]. The initial redox couple (LB/ES) occurs through an anion doping/dedoping process, while the second couple (EB/PS) represents the oxidation and reduction transitions via anion doping and dedoping.

The enclosed areas of the CV curves reflects the total charge storage capacity, which includes contributions from diffusion-controlled and capacitance-controlled processes [29]. The presence of distinct redox peaks suggests a typical pseudocapacitive behavior for CQD@PANI-2. To analyze the dynamic behavior of charge transfer in depth, capacitance-controlled contribution can be estimated via the power law relation i = a × vb, where a and b are adjustable parameters. When the value of b is equal to 0.5, it indicates a diffusion controlled behavior, while when the value of b is equal to 1.0, it indicates a capacitance controlled behavior [30]. The calculated b values for the four redox peaks (Fig. S9) are 0.916, 0.896, 0.857, and 0.943, confirming that the charge storage mechanism in CQD@PANI-2 is primarily pseudocapacitive with a notable contribution from capacitance-controlled process.

By computation, the colored portion of the CV curve represents the contribution of capacitance-controlled processes at 10 mV/s (Fig. 3b), while Fig. 3c displays the contributions of diffusion-controlled and capacitance-controlled processes at various scan rates. As the scan rates increase, the proportion of capacitance-controlled contribution gradually rises, indicating the excellent electrochemical reversibility of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode and demonstrating that capacitance dominated the charge storage process. The stored energy of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode was evaluated by GCD tests at diverse conditions (Fig. 3d). Evidently, GCD curves exhibit a symmetric triangular shape, effectively demonstrating fast charge-storage kinetics [31]. After calculation, the CQD@PANI-2 electrode showcases a maximum specific capacitance of 398.2 F/g at 1 A/g, with a voltage from 0 V to 0.8 V and an area capacitance of 47.7 mF/cm2. Notably, the capacitance retention maintains at 75 % (298.65 F/g) of its original value even at a high current density of 20 A/g (Fig. 3e). It benefits from rapid charge-storage processes and indicated excellent rate capability.

To further reveal the superior capacitance of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode, additional cycling stability tests were performed under high current density conditions to assess its potential for practical applications. The cycling stability of pseudocapacitors is often unsatisfactory due to the negative effects of repeated redox reactions on the active material, which results in the formation of inert by-products [32]. Moreover, under fast charging and discharging conditions, the lifespan of the device is significantly affected as high current densities can undermine the internal structural stability. Fig. 3f shows that CQD@PANI-2 achieves 85 % of capacitance retention after 3000 GCD cycles at 10 A/g, with nearly 100 % coulombic efficiency, illustrating the nanocomposite's excellent cycling stability. Furthermore, the SEM images of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode before and after 5000 GCD cycling are shown in Fig. S10. The CQD@PANI-2 active material maintains the fibrous structure and the porous three-dimensional conductive network without obvious damage and collapse after cycling, which is basically consistent with that before cycling. This result further confirms the CQD@PANI-2 nanocomposite excellent stability performance allowing the practical applications. Based on the SEM images and Raman spectra analysis, the introduction of CQDs greatly alters the conjugated structure and micro-morphology of PANI. The higher doping state induced by CQDs effectively improves both the electrochemical and basic capacitance performance of PANI. Meanwhile, the formation of porous conductive network shortens the diffusion pathway of electrolyte ion and promotes the charge transfer in the nanocomposite, significantly enhancing the rate capability. The existence of porous morphology weakens volume expansion resulted from the insertion/extraction of electrolyte ions in the process of electrochemical reaction, making the connection of active material and substances more stable [33].

The transmittance curves of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode were assessed using a UV–vis–NIR spectrometer. The color of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode changes from light green to dark blue when the potential changed from −0.4 V to 1.0 V (Fig. 4a). Correspondingly, the optical contrast (ΔT) of CQD@PANI-2 reaches about 65 % at 600 nm. The ΔE∗ value is determined by the different value of the CIE L∗ a∗ b∗ color coordinate [34]. On the basis of the CIE 1931 standard, the CQD@PANI-2 electrode changes from its bleached state (L∗ = 57.07, a∗ = 5.37, b∗ = −28.30) to its colored state (L∗ = 66.51, a∗ = −0.75, b∗ = 12.13), with a ΔE∗ of 41.96 (Fig. 4c). This significant change in color indicates that the capacitance variations of CQD@PANI-2 can be effectively distinguished by the naked eyes. Notably, the switching speed defined as the duration required to achieve 90 % fluctuation in transmittance were calculated to be 1.45 s/1.67 s at 600 nm (Fig. 4d). The fast switching speed benefits from the porous conductive network structure of CQD@PANI-2, which facilitates the injection/extraction of electrolytes and the transmission of electrons, as mentioned earlier. Furthermore, the coloration efficiency (CE) is a critical parameter to evaluate the combined efficiency of electrochromic materials [35]. According to the relationship between ΔOD and charge density, the CE value of 45.2 cm2/C at 600 nm surpasses that of many reported studies [36–38], indicating efficient utilization of electrons and strong capability for optical modulation (Fig. 4e).

Fig. 4f presents the GCD curves and the in-situ spectrum of the CQD@PANI-2 electrode, where the ΔT reaches 60 % at 600 nm during the charging-discharging process at 1 A/g. This verifies that the visible changes in the appearance of the electrode can effectively reflect its energy state in a predictable manner. Owing to the CQD@PANI-2 material performed well regarding capacitance and electrochromism, we compared it systematically with similar work in terms of comprehensive performance (Table S2). By contrast, this work presents outstanding overall performance and lays a solid foundation for the subsequent construction of efficient dual-function devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and electrochromic properties of ECSC
ce:section.ce:para: To construct highly efficient electrochromic supercapacitor devices (ECSC), it is critical to select counter electrode materials that are compatible with the CQD@PANI-2 electrode in terms of both charge storage capability and electrochromic behavior. Given these requirements, V2O5@PEDOT emerges as an ideal candidate due to its effective ion storage capacity and high transparency. The electrochemical performance of the V2O5@PEDOT electrode was investigated. The V2O5@PEDOT electrode demonstrates a high area capacitance consistent with the CQD@PANI-2 electrode (Fig. S11a). The diverse CV curves of the V2O5@PEDOT electrode maintain the similar line shapes at various scan rates and it achieves 79.8 % of capacitance retention spanned from 0.1 to 1 mA/cm2, proving an excellent rate capability that allows the rapid performance in the electrochemical reactions. (Figs. S11b and c). In respect of electrochromism, the V2O5@PEDOT electrode possesses a high optical transmittance (Fig. S11d) and it merely appears faint color change at various voltages (Figs. S11e and f). Accordingly, the V2O5@PEDOT electrode was used as a transparent ion storage layer for constructing the efficient devices. The detailed materials characterization of V2O5@PEDOT is provided in Fig. S12.

The ECSC was then assembled using CQD@PANI-2 as the positive electrode, V2O5@PEDOT as the counter electrode, and a gel electrolyte. To identify an optimal voltage window for the asymmetric ECSC, the CV curves of the individual CQD@PANI-2 and V2O5@PEDOT electrodes were measured in a 1 M LiClO4/PC electrolyte. The potential window for CQD@PANI-2 spanned from −0.4V to 1.0 V, while that for V2O5@PEDOT spanned from −0.8 to 0.6 V (Fig. 5 a). The similarity in the integral areas of their respective CV curves suggests that the two electrodes have nearly equivalent capacitance values [39] Further, the exchange charge quantities of CQD@PANI and V2O5@PEDOT in a CV cycle were calculated as 231.2 and 235.7 mC, respectively, which can guarantee the internal charge balance for ECSC [40]. These results demonstrate the capacitive property of positive electrode material matches well with that of negative one, which is conducive to achieve a high-performance ECSC. Withing a voltage range of 0 V–1.2 V, the CV curve of ECSC exhibits a symmetric shape with distinct redox peaks. When the operating voltage is increased to 1.3 V or 1.5 V, leakage current is observed during the discharge process, indicating structural of the ECSC (Fig. 5b) [41]. Therefore, the reasonable operating voltage of the ECSC is determined to range from 0 V to 1.2 V. The diverse CV curves of the device maintain similar shape spanned from 10 mV/s to 100 mV/s, certifying outstanding rate capability and the ability to perform rapid, reversible faradaic redox reactions (Fig. 5c).

To further investigate the capacitive behavior of the ECSC, GCD tests were conducted spanned from 0.1 mA/cm2 to 1 mA/cm2. The maximum area capacitance of the ECSC is 20.1 mF/cm2 at 0.1 mA/cm2, surpassing the performance of many previously reported electrochromic energy storage devices (Fig. 5d) [42–44]. Impressively, the ECSC achieves 73.5 % of capacitance retention when the current density is increased to 1 mA/cm2, underscoring its excellent rate capability (Fig. 5e). These findings, supported by both CV and GCD data, confirm the outstanding electrochemical reversibility and energy storage performance of the ECSC. In term of stability, the ECSC attains 81.2 % of area capacitance retention after 5000 GCD cycles at 1 mA/cm2, with near-perfect coulombic efficiency (close to 100 %), further highlighting its long-term durability and potential for practical application (Fig. 5f).

The electrochromic performance of the ECSC was evaluated through its transmittance curves and visual color changes at various voltages. When applied at −1.2 V, the ECSC appears light yellow with high transmittance. As the voltage increases, the color gradually deepens, changing to yellow-green (0 V) and finally to dark-blue (1.2 V) (Fig. 6 a). The switching time of the device defined as the related time to realize 90 % optical contrast is an important indicator to estimate the electrochromic properties of ECSC [45]. Based on the chronoamperometry (CA) curve and spectrum (Fig. 6b), the result for the coloring and bleaching processes are 0.75 s and 2.61 s, respectively. The coloring efficiency (CE) of the ECSC, determined from the slope of the optical density versus charge density curve, is 221.6 cm2/C (Fig. 6c), indicating superior electrochromic efficiency compared to other reported devices [46,47]. These results confirm that the ECSC delivers excellent performance in both capacitance and electrochromism.

In addition to its exceptional capacitance and electrochromic properties, the ultrathin ECSC demonstrates excellent area power and energy density, ranging from 51.87 μWcm−2 to 4.02 μWhcm−2 to 980.41 μWcm−2 and 2.91 μWhcm−2. When compared to previously reported multifunctional ECSCs, the performance of this device is clearly superior (Fig. 6d) [48–52]. Impressively, the design of this ECSC allows dynamic color changes to be observed and reflects the energy storage state in the form of color. The relationship between energy storage/release and color change during electrochemical processes was analyzed using CIE chromaticity coordinates and ΔE∗ converted from the in-situ spectrum during charging-discharging cycles at 1 A/g (Fig. 6e and f) [53]. The CIE chromaticity coordinates provides a detailed representation of the ECSC's color variations throughout the charging-discharging process, showcasing a clear shift from light yellow to dark blue. The corresponding color difference (ΔE∗) reaches 53.24 at 700 nm (Fig. S13), significantly higher than that of many reported systems [54–56]. This large ΔE∗ value further illustrates that the color changes of the ECSC could effectively indicate its energy storage level during the charging-discharging process.

In comparison to other fabrication methods like magnetron sputtering, hydrothermal techniques, electrochemical deposition, and casting, the spray-coating technique offers a more convenient, rapid, and scalable approach, especially beneficial for large-area and patterned production of ECSC. To explore the potential for practical applications, we fabricated an aesthetically pleasing patterned ECSC to demonstrate its utility in real-world scenarios. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, both CQD@PANI-2 and V2O5@PEDOT electrodes were designed in the shape of a four-leaf clover, creating an artistic and functional large-area patterned ECSC. Upon applying a voltage of −1.2V, the patterned ECSC displays a faint yellow color. As the voltage gradually increases, the device transitions from yellow to green, and finally to a deep blue at 1.2 V, vividly demonstrating dynamic electrochromic behavior (Fig. 7c). The entire coloring and bleaching process can be observed in real-time in Video S1.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2025.236407

The following are the Supplementary data related to this article.
Video S1 Video S1

The patterned ECSC provides a clear visual indicator of the energy storage level through its color changes during the electrochemical process, which mirrors the relationship between stored energy and visual appearance as discussed in section 3.4. This design feature not only enhances the user experience by offering a direct and intuitive way to monitor the device's charge state but also highlights the practical significance of combining electrochromic and energy storage functions. To deeply analyze practical application of the ECSC, it was tested as a power source for a red LED light (requiring 1.8 V). By connecting two ECSCs in series, the red LED was successfully powered for over 62 s, indicating the feasibility of these devices in powering small electronics (Fig. 7b).

These results underscore the great potential of the patterned ECSC for practical use, particularly in applications requiring both energy storage and visual monitoring of charge states. The rapid and scalable spray-coating method also opens new avenues for large-scale production, enabling the creation of customizable, aesthetically pleasing devices that combine functionality with design.

In summary, we successfully prepared a fibrous CQD@PANI nanocomposite through in-situ oxidation polymerization, creating a material with combined electrochemical and electrochromic functionalities. The CQD@PANI nanocomposite forms a porous three-dimensional conductive network, significantly enhancing the rapid transfer of electrolyte ions and charges. This unique structure contributes to the superior electrochemical performance, enabling the material to be a key component in the high-efficiency solid-state ECSC.

Using a scalable spray-coating technique and coupling the CQD@PANI with an optimized counter electrode material (V2O5@PEDOT), we fabricated a highly efficient and portable solid-state ECSC that exhibited excellent performance and was suitable for large-area patterning. The ECSC demonstrates an area capacitance of 20.1 mF/cm2 at 0.1 mA/cm2, along with outstanding rate capability. Furthermore, the device presents distinct color transitions during the charging-discharging process, allowing users to visually monitor the energy storage level in real-time. This seamless integration of energy storage and electrochromism provides a highly practical and user-friendly approach to multifunctional energy devices.

The patterned ECSC, with its combination of rapid charge storage, excellent cycling stability, and real-time color-based energy monitoring, highlights its potential for practical applications in multifunctional electronics, smart energy displays, and portable energy storage systems. This work offers a promising and efficient strategy for the scalable production of high-performance ECSC, opening new avenues for innovative applications in energy-efficient displays and smart devices.

Biao Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Qin Yang: Validation, Formal analysis. Yuanhaobo Yang: Methodology, Investigation. Jia Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Bin Yan: Validation, Formal analysis. Yingchun Gu: Validation, Formal analysis. Runfang Fu: Validation, Formal analysis. Sheng Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Sichuan Province Science and Technology Foundation (2020YFG0094). The authors would like to thank Hui Wang in the Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University for SEM tests, and Shiyanjia Lab (https://www.shiyanjia.com/) for the XRD analysis.
